---
title: "Data science walkthrough 1: Conditionals"
format:
  revealjs:
    toc: false
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---




## A small manuscript problem

We are going to answer one concrete question with the measles data:

> In Pakistan since 2000, which years should we flag for follow-up because first-dose measles vaccine coverage was low and measles incidence was elevated?

This is a miniature version of the Exercise 1 manuscript arc:

1. State a question.
1. Translate the question into a data rule.
1. Run the rule.
1. Write a cautious result.

## The programming idea

We can describe the rule in plain English:

> If MCV1 coverage is below 90% and measles incidence is at least 1 case per 100,000 people, call it a priority year.

Now the problem is not "Can I memorize R?"

The problem is:

> How do I turn that sentence into computer code?

## Load the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data_file <- file.path("data", "measles_final.Rds")

if (!file.exists(data_file)) {
  data_file <- file.path("..", "data", "measles_final.Rds")
}

measles <- readRDS(data_file)
```
:::




## Look at the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
str(measles)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Classes 'spec_tbl_df', 'tbl_df', 'tbl' and 'data.frame':	9416 obs. of  9 variables:
 $ iso3c        : chr  "AFG" "AFG" "AFG" "AFG" ...
 $ year         : num  2023 2022 2021 2020 2019 ...
 $ measles_cases: num  2792 5166 2900 640 353 ...
 $ population   : num  42239854 41128771 40099462 38972230 37769499 ...
 $ MCV1_coverage: num  NA 68 63 66 64 71 67 64 62 60 ...
 $ MCV2_coverage: num  NA 49 44 43 41 49 40 40 42 44 ...
 $ country      : chr  "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" ...
 $ region       : chr  "Asia" "Asia" "Asia" "Asia" ...
 $ sub_region   : chr  "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" "Southern Asia" ...
 - attr(*, "spec")=List of 3
  ..$ cols   :List of 6
  .. ..$ iso3c        : list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_character" "collector"
  .. ..$ year         : list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_double" "collector"
  .. ..$ measles_cases: list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_double" "collector"
  .. ..$ population   : list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_double" "collector"
  .. ..$ MCV1_coverage: list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_double" "collector"
  .. ..$ MCV2_coverage: list()
  .. .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_double" "collector"
  ..$ default: list()
  .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "collector_guess" "collector"
  ..$ delim  : chr ","
  ..- attr(*, "class")= chr "col_spec"
 - attr(*, "problems")=<pointer: (nil)> 
```


:::
:::




## What do we need?

For this question, we need:

- `country`, because we are starting with Pakistan
- `year`, because we only want 2000 and later
- `measles_cases`, because we need incidence
- `population`, because raw case counts are hard to compare
- `MCV1_coverage`, because that is our coverage variable

## Step 1: keep the country-years we need




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan <- measles[
  measles$country == "Pakistan" & measles$year >= 2000,
]

pakistan <- pakistan[order(pakistan$year), ]
```
:::




## Check the subset




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(pakistan)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     iso3c year measles_cases population MCV1_coverage MCV2_coverage  country
6448   PAK 2000          2064  154369924            57            NA Pakistan
6447   PAK 2001          3849  159217727            59            NA Pakistan
6446   PAK 2002          3903  163262807            60            NA Pakistan
6445   PAK 2003          4740  166876680            62            NA Pakistan
6444   PAK 2004          4248  170648620            63            NA Pakistan
6443   PAK 2005          2981  174372098            61            NA Pakistan
     region    sub_region
6448   Asia Southern Asia
6447   Asia Southern Asia
6446   Asia Southern Asia
6445   Asia Southern Asia
6444   Asia Southern Asia
6443   Asia Southern Asia
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
tail(pakistan)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      iso3c year measles_cases population MCV1_coverage MCV2_coverage country
NA.18  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
NA.19  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
NA.20  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
NA.21  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
NA.22  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
NA.23  <NA>   NA            NA         NA            NA            NA    <NA>
      region sub_region
NA.18   <NA>       <NA>
NA.19   <NA>       <NA>
NA.20   <NA>       <NA>
NA.21   <NA>       <NA>
NA.22   <NA>       <NA>
NA.23   <NA>       <NA>
```


:::
:::




## Step 2: calculate incidence

Raw case counts are useful, but they do not account for population size.

So we make a new variable:

> measles cases per 100,000 people




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan$cases_per_100k <- pakistan$measles_cases / pakistan$population * 100000
```
:::




## Check the calculation




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan_check <- pakistan[, c(
  "year",
  "measles_cases",
  "population",
  "cases_per_100k"
)]

pakistan_check$cases_per_100k <- round(pakistan_check$cases_per_100k, 2)

tail(pakistan_check, 8)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      year measles_cases population cases_per_100k
NA.16   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.17   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.18   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.19   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.20   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.21   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.22   NA            NA         NA             NA
NA.23   NA            NA         NA             NA
```


:::
:::




## Step 3: translate each condition

The English rule has two parts:

1. MCV1 coverage is below 90%.
1. Measles incidence is at least 1 case per 100,000 people.

In R, each part becomes a logical vector.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
low_coverage <- pakistan$MCV1_coverage < 90
elevated_incidence <- pakistan$cases_per_100k >= 1
```
:::




## Inspect the logical vectors




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
logic_check <- data.frame(
  year = pakistan$year,
  MCV1_coverage = pakistan$MCV1_coverage,
  cases_per_100k = round(pakistan$cases_per_100k, 2),
  low_coverage = low_coverage,
  elevated_incidence = elevated_incidence
)

tail(logic_check, 8)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   year MCV1_coverage cases_per_100k low_coverage elevated_incidence
41   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
42   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
43   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
44   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
45   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
46   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
47   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
48   NA            NA             NA           NA                 NA
```


:::
:::




## Missing values matter

If coverage is missing, R cannot decide whether coverage is below 90%.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
table(low_coverage, useNA = "ifany")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
low_coverage
TRUE <NA> 
  23   25 
```


:::
:::




That is not a failure. It is R refusing to guess.

For a manuscript-style analysis, we need to decide what we want to do with
missing information.

## Step 4: write the decision rule

Here is the rule in full:

1. If coverage or incidence is missing, label the row `"missing information"`.
1. Otherwise, if coverage is below 90% and incidence is at least 1, label the
   row `"priority year"`.
1. Otherwise, label the row `"not priority"`.

## Step 4: code the decision rule




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan$priority_year <- ifelse(
  is.na(pakistan$MCV1_coverage) | is.na(pakistan$cases_per_100k),
  "missing information",
  ifelse(
    pakistan$MCV1_coverage < 90 & pakistan$cases_per_100k >= 1,
    "priority year",
    "not priority"
  )
)
```
:::




## What did `ifelse()` do?

`ifelse()` has three pieces:

```r
ifelse(test, value_if_true, value_if_false)
```

Our nested version says:

- first check whether the data are missing
- if not, check whether the year meets the priority rule
- give every row one label

## Step 5: inspect the answer




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan_result <- pakistan[, c(
  "year",
  "measles_cases",
  "MCV1_coverage",
  "cases_per_100k",
  "priority_year"
)]

pakistan_result$cases_per_100k <- round(pakistan_result$cases_per_100k, 2)

tail(pakistan_result, 12)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      year measles_cases MCV1_coverage cases_per_100k       priority_year
NA.12   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.13   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.14   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.15   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.16   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.17   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.18   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.19   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.20   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.21   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.22   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.23   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
```


:::
:::




## Step 6: summarize the answer




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
table(pakistan$priority_year)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

missing information        not priority       priority year 
                 25                   5                  18 
```


:::
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan$year[pakistan$priority_year == "priority year"]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] 2000 2001 2002 2003 2004 2005 2006 2007 2010 2011 2012 2013 2016 2017 2018
[16] 2020 2021 2022
```


:::
:::




## Make a small manuscript table




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
priority_years <- pakistan$year[pakistan$priority_year == "priority year"]

data.frame(
  country = "Pakistan",
  start_year = min(pakistan$year),
  end_year = max(pakistan$year),
  total_years = nrow(pakistan),
  priority_years = length(priority_years),
  most_recent_priority_year = max(priority_years)
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   country start_year end_year total_years priority_years
1 Pakistan         NA       NA          48             18
  most_recent_priority_year
1                      2022
```


:::
:::




## Make a quick figure




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(
  pakistan$year,
  pakistan$cases_per_100k,
  type = "b",
  xlab = "Year",
  ylab = "Measles cases per 100,000 people",
  main = "Pakistan measles incidence since 2000"
)

abline(h = 1, lty = 2)

points(
  pakistan$year[pakistan$priority_year == "priority year"],
  pakistan$cases_per_100k[pakistan$priority_year == "priority year"],
  pch = 19,
  col = "red"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Walkthrough1_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## What goes in the report?

**Methods sentence**

We defined a priority year as a country-year with MCV1 coverage below 90% and
at least 1 reported measles case per 100,000 people; country-years with missing
coverage or incidence were labeled as missing information.

**Results sentence**

Pakistan had several priority years since 2000 under this rule, with the most
recent priority year identified in the summary table.

## Active checkpoint

**Student prompt**

Change the question:

> In Afghanistan since 2010, which years should be flagged if MCV2 coverage was
> below 80% or measles incidence was at least 5 cases per 100,000 people?

Write the code before looking at the solution.

## Active checkpoint: hint

You need to change four pieces:

1. the country
1. the start year
1. the coverage variable and cutoff
1. the logical connector, because this rule uses "or"

The R operator for "or" is `|`.

## Active checkpoint: solution




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
afghanistan <- measles[
  measles$country == "Afghanistan" & measles$year >= 2010,
]

afghanistan <- afghanistan[order(afghanistan$year), ]
afghanistan$cases_per_100k <- afghanistan$measles_cases / afghanistan$population * 100000

afghanistan$priority_year <- ifelse(
  (!is.na(afghanistan$MCV2_coverage) & afghanistan$MCV2_coverage < 80) |
    (!is.na(afghanistan$cases_per_100k) & afghanistan$cases_per_100k >= 5),
  "priority year",
  ifelse(
    is.na(afghanistan$MCV2_coverage) | is.na(afghanistan$cases_per_100k),
    "missing information",
    "not priority"
  )
)

afghanistan_result <- afghanistan[, c(
  "year",
  "measles_cases",
  "MCV2_coverage",
  "cases_per_100k",
  "priority_year"
)]

afghanistan_result$cases_per_100k <- round(afghanistan_result$cases_per_100k, 2)

afghanistan_result
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      year measles_cases MCV2_coverage cases_per_100k       priority_year
14    2010          1989            29           7.06       priority year
13    2011          3013            31          10.30       priority year
12    2012          2787            33           9.15       priority year
11    2013           430            38           1.36       priority year
10    2014           492            44           1.50       priority year
9     2015          1154            42           3.42       priority year
8     2016           638            40           1.84       priority year
7     2017          1511            40           4.24       priority year
6     2018          2012            49           5.48       priority year
5     2019           353            41           0.93       priority year
4     2020           640            43           1.64       priority year
3     2021          2900            44           7.23       priority year
2     2022          5166            49          12.56       priority year
1     2023          2792            NA           6.61       priority year
NA      NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.1    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.2    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.3    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.4    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.5    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.6    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.7    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.8    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.9    NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.10   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.11   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.12   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
NA.13   NA            NA            NA             NA missing information
```


:::
:::




## Active checkpoint: summarize it




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
table(afghanistan$priority_year)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

missing information       priority year 
                 14                  14 
```


:::
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
afghanistan$year[afghanistan$priority_year == "priority year"]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023
```


:::
:::




## What did we practice?

- Starting with a question that a reader can understand
- Turning a plain-English rule into logical conditions
- Using `&`, `|`, `is.na()`, and `ifelse()`
- Creating a table, figure, and short manuscript-style interpretation

Next time, we will repeat this same rule across many countries.

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
